sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 72g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for nuts, fat quality has to justify the calorie density.
sections.subtitleText: 72g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for nuts, fat quality has to justify the calorie density.

sections.title: Carbs
sections.narration: 13.9g of carbs. fibre is doing a lot of the work here. for a nut, you need enough upside to make the density worth it.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: 9.2g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is support here, not the whole argument.
sections.subtitleText: 9.2g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is support here, not the whole argument.

sections.title: Vitamins
sections.narration: vitamin e at 10% daily value stands out most. that is barely moving the needle.

sections.title: Minerals
sections.narration: magnesium at 34% daily value, zinc at 29% daily value, iron at 14% daily value stand out most. useful, but still not a major mineral edge.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: the upsides first: portable, practical snack food because nuts score real points in life because they are easy to use. fat and texture help satiety because they can hold up better than many snack foods. still a recognisable whole-food option because that keeps them cleaner than most processed snacks.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: on the weaker side: calorie density is very high because that makes portion control a real issue. protein is useful but not elite because nuts help, but they are not protein stars. cost or allergy issues can limit use because practical downsides still count.

closing.summary: Pecans has a genuinely strong overall case. the practical downsides are real, but they do not outweigh the upside. best in small amounts where the fats and minerals matter more than sheer calories.
